title: Hazelnut praline
subtitle: Caramelised hazelnuts set in amber sugar, then ground to a paste or left as shards
excerpt: Praline is hazelnuts (or almonds) coated in hard amber caramel and set on a oiled surface. Once cold and brittle, it can be left as shards for decoration or ground in a food processor to a praline paste, which is the base for praline buttercream and a great stir-in for ice cream and chocolate ganache.
sourceNotes: Praline made from caramelised sugar and nuts is a classic French confectionery preparation documented from the seventeenth century. The technique of coating whole nuts in hard-crack amber caramel and setting the mixture on an oiled surface is described in domestic confectionery manuals from the nineteenth century, including Mrs A.B. Marshall's works of the 1880s, 1890s and in French domestic cooking guides widely translated into English. The method of grinding set praline to a paste for use in buttercream and ganache is standard in English and French confectionery references and is fully public-domain.

content.text: Caramel at the hard-crack stage is extremely hot. Do not touch it until fully set. If caramel contacts skin, cool under running cold water for at least ten minutes.

Praline paste is worth the fifteen minutes it takes. Stirred into buttercream in place of vanilla, it makes the best frosting for a chocolate or coffee cake. As shards, it decorates a tart or a pavlova without fuss.

Spread the {{caster-sugar}} in an even layer in a medium, heavy saucepan. Heat on medium without stirring; swirl the pan gently as the edges melt. When the caramel is a rich amber (around 160°C), add all the {{hazelnuts}} at once and stir quickly to coat.
Pour onto a mat, cool completely

Pour the praline immediately onto a silicone mat or lightly oiled baking tray. Spread thin with a spatula. Leave to cool completely until brittle and fully hard, about 20 minutes. Break into irregular shards.
, break the praline into chunks and process in a food processor for 3 to 5 minutes, scraping down every minute, until a smooth, pourable paste forms. Store in a sealed jar in the fridge.
